questionText: A 180-metre long train crosses another 270-metre long train running in the opposite direction in 10.8 seconds. If the speed of the first train is 60 kmph, what is the speed of the second train in kmph?

steps.explanation: Convert speed of first train to m/sec.
For two trains moving in opposite directions, relative speed = sum of their speeds. Let speed of second train = x m/sec. Relative speed = (50/3 + x) m/sec.
Total distance = sum of lengths of both trains = 180 + 270 = 450 m. Use: distance = speed × time.
Solve for x.
Convert x to kmph.

solution.shortcut: Total distance / time gives relative speed. Subtract known speed to get unknown speed, then convert.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to add train lengths for total distance when trains cross each other.

modelName: Trains crossing in opposite direction – find unknown speed

questionText: Two trains of equal lengths take 10 seconds and 15 seconds respectively to cross a telegraph post. If the length of each train be 120 metres, in what time (in seconds) will they cross each other travelling in opposite direction?

steps.explanation: Speed of first train = length / time to cross a post.
Speed of second train.
Relative speed when travelling in opposite direction = sum of speeds.
Total distance to cover = sum of lengths = 120 + 120 = 240 m. Time = distance / relative speed.

solution.shortcut: Speed = length / time-to-cross-post. Then time to cross = sum of lengths / sum of speeds (opposite direction).
solution.commonMistake: Using only one train's length instead of both.

modelName: Trains crossing each other – given time to cross post

questionText: A train 108 m long moving at a speed of 50 km/hr crosses a train 112 m long coming from opposite direction in 6 seconds. The speed of the second train is

steps.explanation: Let speed of second train = x km/hr. Relative speed (opposite direction) = (x + 50) km/hr.
Total distance = 108 + 112 = 220 m.
Use time = distance / speed: 6 = 220 / [5(x+50)/18].
Solve for x.

solution.shortcut: Set up: relative speed × time = total length. Solve for unknown speed.
solution.commonMistake: Adding speeds before converting; be careful to convert km/hr to m/sec or work consistently.

modelName: Trains crossing in opposite direction – find unknown speed

questionText: A train B speeding with 120 kmph crosses another train C running in the same direction, in 2 minutes. If the lengths of the trains B and C be 100 m and 200 m respectively, what is the speed (in kmph) of the train C?

steps.explanation: Convert time to seconds: 2 minutes = 120 seconds. Total distance = 100 + 200 = 300 m.
Relative speed = distance / time (same direction = difference of speeds).
Convert speed of B to m/sec: 120 kmph = 100/3 m/sec.
Let speed of train C = x m/sec. Relative speed = v_B - x (same direction, B is faster). Solve for x.
Convert x to kmph.

solution.shortcut: Relative speed (same direction) = difference of speeds. Use total distance and time to find relative speed, then solve.
solution.commonMistake: Using sum instead of difference of speeds for same-direction crossing.

modelName: Trains crossing in same direction – find unknown speed

questionText: One local and another express train were proceeding in the same direction on parallel tracks at 29 km/hr and 65 km/hr respectively. The driver of the former noticed that it took exactly 16 seconds for the faster train to pass by him. What is the length of the faster train?

steps.explanation: Relative speed of express w.r.t. local (same direction) = 65 - 29 = 36 km/hr.
Convert to m/sec.
The driver is a point, so distance covered = length of faster (express) train. Length = relative speed × time.

solution.shortcut: When a train passes a person/driver, distance = length of passing train. Use relative speed × time.
solution.commonMistake: Using absolute speed instead of relative speed.

modelName: Train passing a moving observer – find train length

questionText: Two trains travel in opposite directions at 36 kmph and 45 kmph and a man sitting in slower train passes the faster train in 8 seconds. The length of the faster train is:

steps.explanation: Relative speed (opposite direction) = 36 + 45 = 81 kmph.
Convert to m/sec.
The man (point observer) sees the faster train pass in 8 sec, so distance = length of faster train.

solution.shortcut: Man is a point observer. Length = relative speed × time.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing which train's length is asked.

modelName: Train passing a moving observer – find train length

questionText: A train overtakes two persons who are walking in the same direction in which the train is going, at the rate of 2 kmph and 4 kmph and passes them completely in 9 and 10 seconds respectively. The length of the train is:

steps.explanation: Convert walking speeds: 2 kmph = 5/9 m/sec, 4 kmph = 10/9 m/sec.
Let train length = x m and train speed = y m/sec. Two equations from passing each person: x = (y - 5/9) × 9 and x = (y - 10/9) × 10.
steps.result: Two equations in x and y

steps.explanation: Equate and solve: 9y - 5 = 10y - 100/9 → y = 100/9 - 5 = 55/9 m/sec.
Substitute y back to find x: x = 9 × (55/9) - 5 = 55 - 5 = 50 m.

solution.shortcut: Set up two equations using: length = (train speed - walker speed) × time. Solve simultaneously.
solution.commonMistake: Not converting kmph to m/sec before setting up equations.

modelName: Train overtaking walkers – find train length

questionText: A train overtakes two persons walking along a railway track. The first one walks at 4.5 km/hr. The other one walks at 5.4 km/hr. The train needs 8.4 and 8.5 seconds respectively to overtake them. What is the speed of the train if both the persons are walking in the same direction as the train?

steps.explanation: Convert walking speeds: 4.5 km/hr = 1.25 m/sec, 5.4 km/hr = 1.5 m/sec.
Let train speed = x m/sec. Length of train = (x - 1.25) × 8.4 = (x - 1.5) × 8.5.
Convert to kmph.

solution.shortcut: Equate length expressions for both persons and solve for train speed.
solution.commonMistake: Not converting speeds before forming equations.

modelName: Train overtaking walkers – find train speed

questionText: Two men are running in the same direction with speeds of 6 km per hour and $7\frac{1}{2}$ km per hour respectively. A train running in the same direction crosses them in 5 sec and $5\frac{1}{2}$ sec respectively. The length and the speed of the train are respectively:
options: A) 22.92 m (approximately) and 22 km per hour
B) 22 m (approximately) and 22.5 km per hour
C) 22.90 m (approximately) and 20.5 km per hour
D) 22.92 m (approximately) and 22.5 km per hour
correctAnswer: D) 22.92 m (approximately) and 22.5 km per hour

steps.explanation: Convert speeds: 6 km/hr = 5/3 m/sec; 7.5 km/hr = 25/12 m/sec.
Let train length = x m and train speed = y m/sec. Equations: x = (y - 5/3) × 5 and x = (y - 25/12) × 11/2.
Convert y to kmph: 25/4 × 18/5 = 22.5 km/hr.

solution.shortcut: Form two equations for length using both runners, solve simultaneously for train speed, then compute length.
solution.commonMistake: Arithmetic errors in fraction manipulation; ensure LCD is used correctly.

modelName: Train overtaking runners – find length and speed

questionText: What is the speed of a train if it overtakes two persons who are walking in the same direction at the rate of $a$ m/s and $(a+1)$ m/s and passes them completely in $b$ seconds and $(b+1)$ seconds respectively?

steps.explanation: Let train speed = y m/s and length = x m. First person: x = b(y - a). Second person: x = (b+1)(y - a - 1).
Expand: by - ba = by - ba - b + y - a - 1.
steps.formulaExpression: by - ba = by - ba - b + y - a - 1

steps.explanation: Therefore speed of train = (a + b + 1) m/s.

solution.shortcut: Equate both length expressions and simplify to get train speed directly.
solution.commonMistake: Sign errors when expanding (b+1)(y-a-1).

modelName: Train overtaking walkers – algebraic speed formula

questionText: Two trains, each 100 m long, moving in opposite directions, cross each other in 8 seconds. If one is moving twice as fast the other, then the speed of the faster train is:

steps.explanation: Let slower train speed = x m/sec, then faster = 2x m/sec. Relative speed = x + 2x = 3x.
Total distance = 100 + 100 = 200 m. Time = 8 sec. So 3x = 200/8 = 25.
Speed of faster train = 2x = 50/3 m/sec. Convert to kmph.

solution.shortcut: Relative speed = total length / time. Express in terms of one variable, solve, then convert.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to multiply individual speed by 2 when finding faster train.

modelName: Trains crossing – speed ratio given

questionText: A 150 m long train crosses a milestone in 15 seconds and a train of same length coming from the opposite direction in 12 seconds. The speed of the other train is

steps.explanation: Speed of first train = 150/15 = 10 m/sec.
Let speed of second train = x m/sec. Relative speed (opposite) = 10 + x. Total distance = 150 + 150 = 300 m.
Solve: x = 180/12 = 15 m/sec.
Convert to kmph: 15 × 18/5 = 54 kmph.

solution.shortcut: Find first train speed from milestone crossing, then use crossing time with both trains to get second speed.
solution.commonMistake: Using only one train's length in total distance.

modelName: Train crossing milestone then another train

questionText: A train moving at 15 m/sec takes 20 seconds to pass a cyclist moving in the same direction as that of the train. How much time will the train need to pass the cyclist, if the cyclist moves in a direction opposite to that of the train and if the speed of the cyclist is 5 m/sec and the length of the cycle is 1 m?

steps.explanation: When cyclist and train move in same direction, relative speed = 15 - 5 = 10 m/sec. In 20 sec, length of train + cycle is covered: (x + 1) = 10 × 20 = 200. So x = 199 m.
Now cyclist moves opposite to train. Relative speed = 15 + 5 = 20 m/sec. Distance = train length + cycle length = 199 + 1 = 200 m.

solution.shortcut: Find train length from same-direction scenario. Use opposite-direction relative speed for new time.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to add cycle length (1 m) to train length.

modelName: Train passing cyclist – direction reversal

questionText: A man standing on a platform finds that a train takes 3 seconds to pass him and another train of the same length moving in the opposite direction takes 4 seconds. The time taken by the trains to pass each other will be

steps.explanation: Let length of each train = x m. Speed of first train = x/3 m/sec. Speed of second train = x/4 m/sec.
They move in opposite directions, so relative speed = x/3 + x/4 = 7x/12.
Total distance = x + x = 2x. Time = 2x / (7x/12) = 2x × 12/(7x) = 24/7 seconds.

solution.shortcut: Express each train's speed using their crossing times for man (point observer), then find crossing time.
solution.commonMistake: Treating the trains as moving in same direction instead of opposite.

modelName: Trains passing man then each other

questionText: A train travelling at 48 kmph completely crosses another train having half its length and travelling in opposite direction at 42 kmph, in 12 seconds. It also passes a railway platform in 45 seconds. The length of the platform is

steps.explanation: Let length of first train = x m. Second train = x/2 m. Relative speed = 48 + 42 = 90 kmph = 25 m/sec.
Total distance = x + x/2 = 3x/2. Time = 12 sec. So 3x/2 = 25 × 12 = 300 → x = 200 m.
Speed of first train = 48 kmph = 40/3 m/sec. In 45 sec, it covers: 40/3 × 45 = 600 m = train length + platform length.
Platform length = 600 - 200 = 400 m.

solution.shortcut: First find train length from crossing scenario, then use platform crossing to find platform length.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to subtract train length from total distance when finding platform length.

modelName: Train crossing another train and platform

questionText: Two trains running in opposite directions cross a man standing on the platform in 27 seconds and 17 seconds respectively and they cross each other in 23 seconds. The ratio of their speeds is:

steps.explanation: Let speeds of trains be x m/sec and y m/sec. Length of first train = 27x, length of second = 17y.
steps.calculation: Length = speed × time to cross man

steps.explanation: When they cross each other (opposite direction): (27x + 17y) / (x + y) = 23.
Solve ratio: x/y = 6/4 = 3/2.

solution.shortcut: Use length = speed × time to cross man, then set up crossing equation to find speed ratio.
solution.commonMistake: Swapping which train crosses in which time.

modelName: Speed ratio from crossing times

questionText: Two trains, 130 and 110 metres long, are going in the same direction. The faster train takes one minute to pass the other completely. If they are moving in opposite directions, they pass each other completely in 3 seconds. Find the speed of the faster train.

steps.explanation: Let speeds of faster and slower trains be x and y m/sec. Total length = 130 + 110 = 240 m.
Same direction: 240 / (x - y) = 60 sec (1 minute). So x - y = 4.
Opposite direction: 240 / (x + y) = 3 sec. So x + y = 80.
Add equations: 2x = 84 → x = 42 m/sec.

solution.shortcut: Set up two equations with sum and difference of speeds, solve for the faster train speed.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing same/opposite direction formula for relative speed.

modelName: Two trains – same and opposite direction crossing

questionText: Two identical trains A and B running in opposite directions at same speed take 2 minutes to cross each other completely. The number of bogies of A are increased from 12 to 16. How much more time would they now require to cross each other?

steps.explanation: Let each train have length x m (for 12 bogies) and speed y m/sec. Original crossing: 2x / (2y) = 120 sec → x = 120y.
New length of train A (16 bogies): length = (16/12)x = 4x/3.
New total length = 4x/3 + x = 7x/3. Relative speed = 2y. New time = (7x/3) / (2y) = 7x/(6y).
Extra time = 140 - 120 = 20 sec.

solution.shortcut: Length is proportional to number of bogies. Increase by ratio 16/12, recalculate time, find difference.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that only train A's length changes, not train B's.

modelName: Identical trains – bogie increase and crossing time

questionText: Two stations A and B are 110 km apart on a straight line. One train starts from A at 7 a.m. and travels towards B at 20 kmph. Another train starts from B at 8 a.m. and travels towards A at a speed of 25 kmph. At what time will they meet?

steps.explanation: Train from A starts at 7 a.m. Let them meet x hours after 7 a.m. Train from B starts 1 hour later, so travels for (x - 1) hours.
Together they cover 110 km: 20x + 25(x-1) = 110.
They meet at 7 + 3 = 10 a.m.

solution.shortcut: Set up combined distance equation. One train starts 1 hr late, so subtract that gap from its travel time.
solution.commonMistake: Assuming both trains start at the same time.

modelName: Meeting point of two trains from different stations

questionText: A train X starts from Meerut at 4 p.m. and reaches Ghaziabad at 5 p.m. while another train Y starts from Ghaziabad at 4 p.m. and reaches Meerut at 5.30 p.m. The two trains will cross each other at

steps.explanation: Let distance between Meerut and Ghaziabad = x km. Speed of X = x kmph (covers x km in 1 hr). Speed of Y = x/(3/2) = 2x/3 kmph (covers x km in 1.5 hr).
steps.calculation: Speed of X = x; Speed of Y = 2x/3

steps.explanation: Let they meet y hours after 4 p.m. Distance covered by X + Y = x. So xy + (2x/3)y = x → y(1 + 2/3) = 1 → y(5/3) = 1 → y = 3/5 hours.
Meeting time = 4:00 + 36 min = 4:36 p.m.

solution.shortcut: Express speeds via journey time, set combined distance = total distance, find meeting time.
solution.commonMistake: Treating the distance as unknown but forgetting it cancels out.

modelName: Meeting time of trains from opposite stations

questionText: The Ghaziabad-Hapur-Meerut EMU and the Meerut-Hapur-Ghaziabad EMU start at the same time from Ghaziabad and Meerut and proceed towards each other at 16 km/hr and 21 km/hr respectively. When they meet, it is found that one train has travelled 60 km more than the other. The distance between two stations is

steps.explanation: Let the first train (16 km/hr) travel x km. Then the second (21 km/hr) travels x + 60 km (since faster one covers more distance).
Since both travel for the same time t: x/16 = (x+60)/21 → 21x = 16x + 960 → 5x = 960 → x = 192 km.
Total distance = x + (x + 60) = 192 + 252 = 444 km.

solution.shortcut: Use ratio of speeds = ratio of distances (same time). Difference in distances = 60 km.
solution.commonMistake: Assigning the extra 60 km to the slower train instead of the faster one.

modelName: Two trains meeting – distance difference given

formulas.title: Same time: ratio of distances = ratio of speeds

questionText: Two trains, one from Howrah to Patna and the other from Patna to Howrah, start simultaneously. After they meet, the trains reach their destinations after 9 hours and 16 hours respectively. The ratio of their speeds is:

steps.explanation: When two trains start simultaneously from opposite ends and meet, the ratio of their speeds equals the square root of the inverse ratio of the times they take to reach their destinations after meeting.
So ratio of speeds = 4 : 3.

solution.shortcut: Speed ratio = √(time_B / time_A) when trains start simultaneously from opposite ends.
solution.commonMistake: Taking direct ratio of times instead of square root of inverse ratio.

modelName: Two trains meeting – speed ratio from post-meeting times

questionText: Two trains start simultaneously (with uniform speeds) from two stations 270 km apart, each to the opposite station; they reach their destinations in $6\frac{1}{4}$ hours and 4 hours after they meet. The rate at which the slower train travels is

steps.explanation: Using the ratio formula: speed ratio = √(post-meeting time of other / post-meeting time of this). Post-meeting times are 25/4 and 4 hours.
Let speeds be 4k and 5k km/hr. Time to meet = 270/(4k + 5k) = 270/(9k) = 30/k hours.

Slower train (4k) travels for 30/k hours before meeting, then 25/4 more hours to destination. Total time for slower = 30/k + 25/4. Total distance = 270 km. Alternatively use: after meeting, slower takes 25/4 hr to cover remaining distance at 4k. So 4k × 25/4 = remaining distance = 25k. And faster takes 4 hr to cover its remaining at 5k → 5k × 4 = 20k. Together: 25k + 20k = 270 → 45k = 270 → k = 6.
Speed of slower train = 4k = 24 km/hr.

solution.shortcut: Use speed ratio formula from post-meeting times, then use total distance to find k.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing which post-meeting time belongs to which train in the ratio formula.

modelName: Two trains meeting – find speed using post-meeting times

questionText: A train which is moving at an average speed of 40 km/h reaches its destination on time. When its average speed reduces to 35 km/h, then it reaches its destination 15 minutes late. The distance travelled by the train is

steps.explanation: Let distance = d km. Normal time = d/40 hr. Late time = d/35 hr. Difference = 15 min = 1/4 hr.

solution.shortcut: Difference in times = distance × (1/v2 - 1/v1). Solve for d.
solution.commonMistake: Not converting 15 minutes to hours (1/4 hr) before solving.

modelName: Distance from speed and delay

questionText: A train takes 9 sec to cross a pole. If the speed of the train is 48 kmph, then length of the train is

steps.explanation: Convert speed: 48 kmph = 48 × 5/18 = 40/3 m/sec.
Length = speed × time = (40/3) × 9 = 120 m.

solution.shortcut: Length = speed (m/s) × time. Convert kmph to m/s first.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting unit conversion from kmph to m/sec.

modelName: Train crossing a pole – find length

questionText: Two trains start at the same time from A and B and proceed toward each other at the speed of 75 km/hr and 50 km/hr respectively. When both meet at a point in between, one train was found to have travelled 175 km more than the other. Find the distance between A and B.

steps.explanation: Both trains travel for the same time t. Faster train (75 km/hr) covers more distance. 75t - 50t = 175 → 25t = 175 → t = 7 hr.
Total distance = (75 + 50) × 7 = 125 × 7 = 875 km.

solution.shortcut: Difference of distances = difference of speeds × time. Find time, then total distance = sum of speeds × time.
solution.commonMistake: Subtracting instead of adding speeds for total distance.

modelName: Two trains meeting – distance difference given

questionText: A train passes two bridges of lengths 500 m and 250 m in 100 seconds and 60 seconds respectively. The length of the train is

steps.explanation: Let train length = x m and speed = v m/sec. Two equations: (x + 500)/v = 100 and (x + 250)/v = 60.
steps.calculation: Divide both equations to eliminate v

solution.shortcut: Take ratio of (train+bridge) distances to eliminate speed, then solve for train length.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to include train length in total distance crossed.

modelName: Train passing two bridges – find train length

questionText: Train A passes a lamp post in 9 seconds and 700 meter long platform in 30 seconds. How much time will the same train take to cross a platform which is 800 meters long? (in seconds)

steps.explanation: Let train length = x m and speed = v. From lamp post: v = x/9.

solution.shortcut: Find train length from lamp post and platform data, then apply to new platform.
solution.commonMistake: Using 700 m as train length instead of solving for it.

modelName: Train crossing platform – find time for different platform

questionText: Train A travelling at 63 kmph can cross a platform 199.5 m long in 21 seconds. How much time would train A take to completely cross (from the moment they meet) train B, 157 m long and travelling at 54 kmph in opposite direction which train A is travelling? (in seconds)

steps.explanation: Speed of A = 63 kmph = 63 × 5/18 = 17.5 m/sec.
Length of train A: v × t = L_A + L_platform → 17.5 × 21 = L_A + 199.5 → L_A = 367.5 - 199.5 = 168 m.
Speed of B = 54 kmph = 54 × 5/18 = 15 m/sec. Relative speed (opposite direction) = 17.5 + 15 = 32.5 m/sec.

solution.shortcut: First find length of A using platform crossing. Then use relative speed and sum of lengths for crossing time.
solution.commonMistake: Not finding train A's length first before computing crossing with B.

modelName: Train crossing platform then another train
